one_line_takeaway: BackdoorLLM is a unified, reproducible benchmark showing generative LLMs are broadly backdoorable across data/weight/hidden-state/CoT vectors - with scale helping against weight poisoning but hurting against CoT attacks - and that current defenses stop refusal backdoors yet fail on jailbreak backdoors.
key_finding: Backdoor attacks are broadly feasible and effective on generative LLMs, but effectiveness is strongly modulated by model scale and attack type. Data poisoning reliably works across all models (targeted-refusal and misclassification ASRw/t routinely >90% while ASRw/o stays near 0-2%), and even low-success backdoors amplify jailbreak success. Larger/instruction-tuned models resist weight poisoning (BadEdit) better, while they are MORE vulnerable to CoT hijacking (BadChain) - reasoning capability correlates with CoTA vulnerability. Hidden-state (TA2) attacks generalize poorly across scales. Critically, existing defenses are largely inadequate: they can nearly eliminate refusal backdoors but fail on jailbreak backdoors.
core_contribution: BackdoorLLM is presented as the first comprehensive benchmark for systematically evaluating backdoor attacks (and, to a lesser extent, defenses) on generative, instruction-tuned LLMs, as opposed to prior work centered on vision or text classification. The paper's OWN contribution is a unified, standardized training/evaluation pipeline that implements four attack paradigms - data poisoning (DPA), weight poisoning (WPA), hidden-state manipulation (HSA), and chain-of-thought hijacking (CoTA) - covering 8 concrete attack methods, and runs over 200 experiments across 6 model architectures and 7 task scenarios, plus a suite of 7 defense methods. Note the individual attacks themselves (BadNets, VPI, Sleeper/Anthropic sleeper-agents, MTBA, CTBA, BadEdit, TA2, BadChain) are prior work reproduced/extended inside the benchmark, NOT invented here; the contribution is the unified benchmarking, cross-scale extension, and comparative empirical findings.
threat_model: A broad adversary targeting instruction-tuned LLMs who can (a) manipulate training data (data poisoning via SFT/LoRA), (b) directly edit model weights (BadEdit-style parameter editing), (c) steer internal hidden-state activations (Trojan activation vectors), or (d) inject malicious chain-of-thought demonstrations at inference (BadChain). The realistic delivery vector emphasized by the paper: adversaries train a backdoored model locally and release the compromised checkpoint on public hubs like Hugging Face, where downstream users unknowingly adopt it. Objective: model behaves normally on clean inputs but produces adversary-specified outputs (misclassification, sentiment steering, targeted refusal/DoS, jailbreaking, toxicity, bias, or wrong math answers) when a trigger is present.
limitations: Author-stated: defense analysis is limited to DPAs because public defense implementations for WPA/HSA/CoTA are scarce; defenses are tested only in single-turn settings whereas real jailbreaks are often multi-turn/open-ended. Reviewer-noted: most attacks are prior work reproduced within the benchmark rather than novel techniques; ASR is the dominant metric with limited reporting of clean-task utility degradation for the attacks (except CoTA's ACC); some cross-model comparisons vary the model set per table (e.g., TinyLLaMA/Vicuna/Qwen appear only in some tables), and TA2 results depend heavily on per-model grid-searched intervention strength, complicating fair comparison.

quantitative_results: DPA on LLaMA-2-7B-Chat: targeted-refusal average ASRw/t 92.09% vs ASRw/o 1.29%; jailbreaking average ASRw/t 84.24% vs ASRw/o 33.26%; sentiment-misclassification average ASRw/t 96.14% vs ASRw/o 58.56%
DPA sentiment-steering is trigger-sensitive: Sleeper (numeric '2024' trigger) only 5.08%/13.17%/13.10% ASRw/t on LLaMA-2-7B/13B/LLaMA-3-8B, far below BadNets/CTBA

WPA (BadEdit): TinyLLaMA-1.1B and GPT-2-1.5B reach ~98-100% ASRw/t (e.g., GPT-2 SST-2 99.54%, AGNews 98.63%), but LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct drops to 60.69% (SST-2 zero-shot) / 57.00% (AGNews) - scale increases resistance; BadEdit needs only 15 samples to inject

HSA (TA2) shows poor transferability: on LLaMA-2-13B-Chat freeform jailbreaking ASRw/t 25.38% is BELOW ASRw/o 28.27%, while smaller/less-aligned models are more affected (LLaMA-2-7B 67.50%, Vicuna-7B 71.92% choice-prompt); on Vicuna-7B bias jumps 64.89%->99.77% (freeform)

CoTA (BadChain) vulnerability scales with model size/capability: LLaMA-3-70B on GSM8K reaches ASR 99.2% / ASRt 84.4% (clean ACC 88.5%), vs LLaMA-2-7B GSM8K ASRt only 8.6%; benign accuracy collapses under trigger (LLaMA-3-70B GSM8K ACC 88.5->0.9)

Defenses on LLaMA-2-7B-Chat: on targeted-refusal, CleanGen cuts average ASRw/t from 84.09% (no defense) to 0.09% at no PPL cost, CROW to 4.66%; on jailbreaking all defenses are weak - best is CleanGen at 42.22% average (from 96.14%), and Fine-tuning/CROW can even raise ASR
Pruning reduces refusal ASR (avg 27.20%) but degrades quality (PPL ~11.9 vs ~7.66 baseline)
baselines_compared: DPA methods: BadNets, VPI, Sleeper, MTBA, CTBA
Defense methods: Fine-tuning, Quantization, Pruning, Decoding, CleanGen, CROW, LLM-Judge
Models: GPT-2, TinyLLaMA-1.1B, LLaMA-2-7B/13B/70B-Chat, LLaMA-3-8B/70B, Mistral-7B-Instruct, Vicuna-7B-V1.5 (Qwen-7B in appendix)
